text: The surgeon inserts a reusable transanal D-port into the anus and removes the obturator, preparing for the transanal endolaparoscopic resection of a rectal polyp located approximately two centimeters from the dentate line.

The surgeon applies a reusable silicone cap and introduces a regular thirty-degree, ten-millimeter scope into the surgical field. A monocurve reusable grasping forceps is inserted through the nine o'clock port orifice.

A graduated straight grasping forceps is inserted at the three o'clock port orifice, and the degenerated rectal polyp is visible less than two centimeters from the dentate line. A monocurve reusable coagulating hook is inserted at the same port orifice, and circumferential scoring is applied around the lesion to ensure safe margins.

The surgeon begins the resection at the six o'clock position using monocurved grasping forceps and a monocurved coagulating hook. The mucosal and submucosal layers are incised, followed by the sectioning of the muscular layers, which exposes the recto-rectal space. The dissection proceeds towards the left lateral side.

The surgeon visualizes the correct plane of dissection and continues incising the remnant rectal wall on the left lateral side. The dissection progresses to the upper part of the rectum using the monocurve coagulating hook.
The surgeon begins dissection on the right lateral side using the full thickness method, clearly visualizing the perirectal fatty tissue, rectal muscular layer, submucosal, and mucosal layers.
The surgeon removes the completely freed specimen through the D-port for pathological evaluation.

The surgeon uses a monocled reusable needle holder to insert an absorbable barbed suture at the three o'clock port orifice. The rectal wall closure begins at the central point, proceeding to the left lateral side. Full thickness bites are placed on the dentate line, with the suture maintained under tension to counteract tissue retraction.

The surgeon continues suturing the rectal wall, reaching the middle segment of the left lateral side. Full thickness bites are taken, and a final intercorporeal knot is tied, maintaining excellent ergonomics similar to conventional laparoscopy.

Monocurve reusable scissors are inserted through the three o'clock port orifice to cut the first suture. A new absorbable barbed suture is initiated at the apex of the right lateral dissection, and the surgeon begins closing the rectal wall using the full thickness method.
The surgeon places subsequent full thickness suture bites, reaching the central point where the previous suture was initiated, ensuring the rectal wall closure is secure.
The surgeon creates a final intercorporeal knot, and the camera reveals the completed suture line within the cavity, showing no openings or flaps.
